title: Troilus and Criseyde
summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00002. The item's title pages identify a 1967 Johnson Reprint of Frederick J. Furnivall's two-part 1881–1882 Chaucer Society parallel-text edition. Furnivall printed independent transcriptions of three manuscript witnesses in parallel columns: the Campsall manuscript, now Morgan MS M.817; British Library Harley MS 2280; and Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27. The XML transforms those columns into sequential witness-version divisions inside each of the poem's five books. No public complete digital facsimile of any of the three physical codices was verified on 2026-07-11: the Morgan exposes a selected opening image, the British Library reports its digital images unavailable, and no Cambridge whole-codex image service was found. Complete-codex imaging may be requested from each repository, and a three-volume printed facsimile of Gg.4.27 exists.
review_notes: The poem, three manuscript objects, Furnivall's edition, Johnson's reprint, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML are modeled as distinct layers.
The manuscript access records distinguish a selected leaf, a full-codex facsimile, and a reproduction request; a selected image is never labeled complete.
Local caching records retrieval and fixity only. It does not authorize redistribution or publication of provider-supplied images.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00002
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: IDG/@ID, BIBNO, and VID all identify CME00002.
The XML contains 873 PB milestones and 10,818 manuscript-leaf or other milestones, but these are locators in a transformed edition rather than images of the manuscripts.

entities.label: University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item CME00002
entities.description: A public structured digital text representing Furnivall's parallel-text edition and its notes.
notes: The browser presentation exposes the complete keyed text and identifies the 1881–1882 edition.
An item-specific downloadable source XML URL was not independently verified.

entities.label: Johnson Reprint of Furnivall's Parallel-Text Print

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde (New York: Johnson Reprint Corporation; London: Johnson Reprint Company Limited, 1967), reprinting the Chaucer Society edition of 1881–1882.
bibliographic.title: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde

notes: The CME XML includes Johnson's reprint statement, making this the strongest identified immediate print layer.
No production documentation was found proving whether U-M keyed directly from this physical copy or from an intermediate file.

entities.label: Furnivall parallel-text edition, 1881–1882

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. of Mr. Bacon Frank, the Harleian MS. 2280, and Cambridge University Library MS. Gg.4.27, Chaucer Society, First Series 63–64, 2 parts (London: N. Trübner, 1881–1882).
bibliographic.title: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde

notes: Part I contains Books I–II, printed pp. 1–102; Part II contains Books III–V, pp. 103–298.
The printed page presents the three transcriptions in adjacent columns, unlike the XML's sequential serialization.

entities.label: Furnivall one-text print from the Campsall manuscript, 1888

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A One-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. (London: Chaucer Society, 1888).
bibliographic.title: A One-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS.

notes: Furnivall states that this Campsall transcription had been typeset before he completed the parallel edition; after comparison he preferred Harley 2280 but issued the already-set Campsall text.

entities.label: Rossetti comparison of Chaucer's Troylus with Boccaccio's Filostrato, Part I

bibliographic.citation: William Michael Rossetti, ed., Chaucer's Troylus and Cryseyde (from Harl. MS. 3943) Compared with Boccaccio's Filostrato, Part I (London: Chaucer Society, 1873).
bibliographic.title: Chaucer's Troylus and Cryseyde (from Harl. MS. 3943) Compared with Boccaccio's Filostrato

notes: The cached object is the complete 1873 Part I, not the later continuation of Rossetti's comparison.

entities.label: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.817
alternate_labels: Campsall MS. of Mr. Bacon Frank
entities.description: The earliest securely datable witness of Troilus and Criseyde and the first column of Furnivall's edition.

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.notes: The Morgan describes it as made in England, probably London, within this securely datable range.

holding.notes: Formerly at Campsall and sold from the F. Bacon Frank estate in 1942 before Morgan acquisition.

notes: Written in secretary with textura; decorated and armorial.
The poem begins on current Morgan fol. 1r. The final current folio is associated with ownership/provenance rather than the poem.
The Morgan calls it the only Chaucer manuscript certainly owned by the English royal family and associates it with the future Henry V; wording is kept short of asserting a proven royal commission.
Furnivall's historical leaf numbering starts at leaf 2 for the surface now labeled fol. 1r. The inferred constant offset through the ending remains to be checked against a complete surrogate.

entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2280
entities.description: A complete-text witness except for one lost leaf in Book V, printed in the second column of Furnivall's parallel edition.

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: first half of the fifteenth century

physical_description.extent: 98 folios, with four paper and one parchment flyleaves at the beginning and six flyleaves at the end
notes: Written in England in Gothic script.
Troilus and Criseyde occupies fols. 1r–98r; one leaf lost between fols. 92 and 93 removes Book V lines 1345–1428.
The work occupies nearly the entire written codex, but a complete physical-object reproduction would also include flyleaves, bindings, and other non-textual surfaces.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27
entities.description: A large Chaucer codex, now bound in two parts, whose fragmentary Troilus and Criseyde transcription forms the third column of Furnivall's edition.

date_statements.label: recent scholarly dating of the principal scribe

holding.notes: The medieval codex and Elizabethan supplied leaves are now bound as two physical parts under the same shelfmark.

physical_description.extent: 517 leaves plus 36 later supplied leaves; 62 whole leaves reported missing
notes: The main medieval Troilus begins at fol. 14 and the Canterbury Tales begins at fol. 132.
The beginnings of all five books and other leaves are missing. Elizabethan replacement leaves are separately bound.
Furnivall transcribes the extant medieval sequence beginning at his leaf 14 and does not silently substitute the later supplied opening.
The broad work region is fols. 14r–131v, with extensive losses; Furnivall's extant transcription ends at fol. 126v because fols. 127–131 are missing.

entities.label: Google Books scan of Furnivall parallel-text edition, Part I
entities.description: A complete scan of the 1881 first part, containing Books I–II and the edition's preliminary matter.
entities.reproduction_format: Digitized printed-volume page images and PDF

entities.label: Google Books scan of Furnivall parallel-text edition, Part II
entities.description: A full-view scan of the 1882 second part, containing Books III–V and printed pages 103–298.
notes: The volume was publicly viewable, but an automated complete PDF retrieval encountered Google's CAPTCHA and was not cached.

entities.label: HathiTrust scan of the 1967 Johnson Reprint
entities.description: Full-view page images of the 298-page Johnson Reprint corresponding to the print layer identified in CME00002.

entities.label: Morgan selected image of MS M.817, fol. 1r
entities.description: A single public color image of the opening written leaf, not a facsimile of the complete manuscript.
notes: This entity is intentionally labeled as a selected leaf. It must not satisfy a complete-manuscript-facsimile flag.

entities.label: Printed facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27
entities.description: The three-volume printed facsimile Poetical Works: A Facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27, with modern introductions by M. B. Parkes and Richard Beadle.

date_statements.label: publication
date_statements.notes: Catalog records checked use 1979–1980 and 1979–1981 date ranges.

bibliographic.citation: Poetical Works: A Facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27, introductions by M. B. Parkes and Richard Beadle, 3 vols. (Cambridge: D. S. Brewer, 1979–1981).
bibliographic.title: Poetical Works: A Facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27

notes: No lawful public digital copy of this modern facsimile was verified. Library catalog and bookseller routes may provide access to the complete publication.

entities.label: M. R. James description of CUL MS Gg.4.27(1)
entities.description: A digitized historical catalog description recording the codex's extent, dimensions, contents, lacunae, and later supplied leaves.

entities.label: U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A
entities.description: Official guidance used only for the United States term analysis of the nineteenth-century publications.

access.access_method: Submit the Morgan Photo Services online application and request the complete physical manuscript rather than only the poem leaves
access.contact: Morgan Library & Museum Photo Services via the online application
access.format: Existing digital images or new photography, subject to condition, availability, quotation, and permissions
notes: Ask for complete codex coverage, including bindings, flyleaves, blank surfaces, and fol. 120; separately identify Troilus as current fols. 1r–approximately 119v.
The Morgan gives an 8–12 week processing estimate and says new photography is considered case by case.

access.access_method: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog
British Library digital item viewer
notes: The current catalog explicitly reports the digital images as unavailable. No partial images are cached as a substitute.

access.access_method: Submit the British Library digitisation order form for photography of the complete physical manuscript
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services via the online order form
access.format: Photography of original manuscripts; exact complete-codex deliverables subject to quotation and condition
notes: Request fols. 1–98, all opening and closing flyleaves, bindings, blanks, and any stubs so the reproduction represents the whole physical object; map the work separately to fols. 1r–98r.
The service describes manuscript capture as photography rather than scanning; feasibility and price require a quote.

notes: Request complete coverage of both presently bound parts, including bindings, flyleaves, blank and supplied leaves; map Furnivall's medieval Troilus transcription separately to fols. 14r–126v and the broad work region to fols. 14r–131v.
Fragile material may be restricted, and reproduction/publication permission is a separate question.

notes: No Copyright–United States is a jurisdictional rights statement, not a worldwide license and not permission for independently supplied manuscript images.

rights.component: Furnivall's 1881–1882 edition text and original editorial matter
rights.basis: The edition was published in 1881–1882, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff described in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
notes: This does not decide copyright or contractual restrictions in modern scans, later reprints, or manuscript photographs.

rights.component: The 1888 edition's underlying text and editorial matter
rights.basis: The publication date is 1888, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
notes: The cached scan includes Google usage guidance concerning the digitized file; preserve file/provider terms separately from the public-domain book.

rights.component: The 1873 comparison edition's underlying text and editorial matter
rights.basis: The publication date is 1873, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff.
notes: Provider and scan-file terms remain distinct from the public-domain source publication.

rights.component: The 1967 reprint as a whole, including any new matter, typography, or production elements
rights.basis: The underlying 1881–1882 edition is public domain, but no copyright-registration, renewal, or new-matter analysis was completed for Johnson's 1967 reprint layer.
notes: Do not treat uncertainty about the reprint's new matter as extending copyright in Chaucer or Furnivall's nineteenth-century edition.

rights.component: Google's digitized Part I file and service delivery

rights.basis: The underlying edition is public domain in the United States, but Google Books usage and service terms may govern the supplied scan file and access route.
notes: Local caching does not establish redistribution permission for the Google-generated PDF.

rights.component: Google's digitized Part II file and service delivery
rights.jurisdiction: Provider terms; underlying edition analyzed separately for the United States
rights.basis: The underlying edition is public domain in the United States, but Google Books usage and service terms may govern the supplied scan file and access route.

rights.component: The Morgan's modern digital photograph of MS M.817 fol. 1r
rights.jurisdiction: Morgan image-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed
rights.basis: The Morgan's imaging page sells images for research and publication and states that unauthorized duplication or dissemination is forbidden; no open license was found.
notes: The medieval text itself is not made copyrighted by the modern photograph; publication of the supplied image is a separate permissions question.

rights.component: Any complete-codex images newly supplied by the Morgan
rights.jurisdiction: Morgan image-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed
rights.basis: The Morgan requires an application, charges image and publication fees, and restricts unauthorized duplication and dissemination.

rights.component: Any complete-codex photographs supplied by British Library Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed
rights.basis: The service terms and rate card distinguish supply of images from permissions for reuse or publication.

rights.component: Any complete-codex images supplied by the CUL Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory
rights.jurisdiction: Cambridge University Library service and licensing terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed
rights.basis: CUL offers bespoke imaging and licensing, with publication and permissions handled through its service process; no open license for requested manuscript images was found.

rights.component: Modern facsimile photographs, layout, and introductions
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and other applicable jurisdictions
rights.basis: The facsimile was published in 1979–1981 with modern photography, introductions, and editorial production; no open license was found.
notes: Access to or purchase of the volumes does not by itself authorize scanning and repository redistribution.

rights.component: The Cambridge repository PDF and its digitized presentation
rights.basis: The historical description is old, but no explicit open license for the repository PDF or its digitization was verified.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Three independent manuscript texts with divergent readings and lacunae
editorial_practices.representation: Campsall, Harley 2280, and Gg.4.27 printed in aligned parallel columns with shared stanza and line coordination
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall preserves three witness streams and aligns them for comparison rather than silently constructing a single eclectic text.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Passages related with different degrees of closeness to Boccaccio's Filostrato
editorial_practices.representation: Three grades of side marks distinguish substantially translated, generally similar, and slightly similar passages
editorial_practices.description: The edition's side marks encode a source-comparison layer derived from William Michael Rossetti's work; they are not manuscript punctuation.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Very small final letters in the manuscripts
editorial_practices.representation: Omitted from the print according to Furnivall's headnote
editorial_practices.description: Furnivall explicitly declines to reproduce very small final letters, so the print and downstream XML are selective even when otherwise diplomatic.
editorial_practices.scope: All three witness transcriptions where the stated convention applies

editorial_practices.source_feature: Probable manuscript abbreviation, contraction, or supplied-letter features at individual loci
editorial_practices.representation: Letters printed in italics and preserved in the XML as HI/@REND=italic
editorial_practices.description: Internal italics often appear where an editor may be expanding or supplying letters, but no comprehensive convention statement was located in the checked preliminary matter. Each locus should be compared with a manuscript image before assigning the exact underlying sign or claiming that italics always have one meaning.
editorial_practices.scope: Internal italic spans in the witness transcriptions
notes: This practice is deliberately cautious: a full manuscript facsimile is required to distinguish expansion, supply, correction, or typographic emphasis at a particular reading.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Three simultaneous printed columns per book
editorial_practices.representation: Separate sequential DIV3 witness-version streams: Campsall version 1, Harley version 2, Gg.4.27 version 3
editorial_practices.description: The digital encoding linearizes the printed parallel layout. Consumers must use the version headings and milestones rather than read the three streams as consecutive intrinsic sections of Chaucer's poem.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00002.xml.
evidence.locator: IDG; title pages; Books I–V; fifteen witness-version DIV3 elements; manuscript and printed-page milestones; notes
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00002; transcribes Johnson's reprint statement and Furnivall's title page; preserves the five books, three witness streams, lacuna notes, typography, and locators; and demonstrates the sequential XML transformation of the parallel print layout.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, ‘A parallel-text print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde,’ CME item CME00002.
evidence.summary: Confirms the public full-text item, Chaucer and Furnivall responsibilities, 1881–1882 Chaucer Society imprint, stable identifier, and No Copyright–United States rights statement.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, Bibliography entry BIB625, ‘Troilus and Criseyde.’
evidence.locator: Entry information, manuscript list, witness stencils, Furnivall edition
evidence.summary: Confirms the two-part 1881–1882 Furnivall edition, three manuscript witnesses and their modern shelfmarks, IMEV/NIMEV 3327 and DIMEV 5249, and attributed dates and dialect information whose scope is kept witness-specific.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde, Part I (Chaucer Society, First Series 63, 1881), Google Books volume 6Ue_hzUjTUEC.
evidence.locator: Title page, editor's headnote, Books I–II, printed pp. 1–102
evidence.summary: Confirms the title and imprint, three-column arrangement, witness headings, Boccaccio/Rossetti side-mark legend, stated treatment of small final letters, manuscript gaps, and printed layout.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde, Part II (Chaucer Society, First Series 64, 1882), Google Books volume JzzTo8sUWAgC.
evidence.summary: Identifies the full-view second part and completes the bibliographic and pagination coverage of Furnivall's edition; the generated PDF could not be cached because Google presented a CAPTCHA.

evidence.citation: HathiTrust catalog and full-view record for A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde (Johnson Reprint, 1967), volume uc1.31210002931200.
evidence.summary: Confirms the New York Johnson Reprint publication date, 298-page extent, and a public full-view digital surrogate corresponding to the reprint statement encoded in CME00002.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A One-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. (London: Chaucer Society, 1888), Internet Archive item aonetextprintch00furngoog.
evidence.locator: Title page; forewords, pp. vii–viii; complete Campsall text
evidence.summary: Furnivall explains the prior typesetting of Campsall, his Boccaccio marking based on Rossetti, and his later judgment that Harley 2280 was the better manuscript.

evidence.citation: William Michael Rossetti, ed., Chaucer's Troylus and Cryseyde (from Harl. MS. 3943) Compared with Boccaccio's Filostrato, Part I (London: Chaucer Society, 1873), Internet Archive item chaucerstroylus00rossgoog.
evidence.summary: Provides the comparison source Furnivall says he used for the degree-of-resemblance side marks; the cached source object is the complete 1873 Part I.

evidence.citation: Morgan Library & Museum, MS M.817, Troilus and Criseyde.
evidence.summary: Catalogues the 120-leaf parchment Campsall witness, probably made in London in 1403–1413, and describes its royal provenance, script, decoration, dimensions, and current shelfmark.

evidence.citation: Morgan Library & Museum manuscript viewer, MS M.817, selected fol. 1r image.
evidence.summary: The selected color image confirms that the current first written leaf opens Troilus and carries armorial decoration. Only one public image was exposed; it does not establish complete digital coverage.

evidence.citation: Morgan Library & Museum, ‘Imaging & Rights.’
evidence.summary: Describes research and publication image orders, fees, an 8–12 week estimate, case-by-case new photography, and restrictions on unauthorized duplication and dissemination.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts, Harley MS 2280.
evidence.locator: Contents, physical description, foliation, digital-content status
evidence.summary: Places Troilus at fols. 1r–98r, records the lost leaf between fols. 92 and 93 and the missing Book V lines, describes the 98-folio parchment codex and flyleaves, dates it to the first half of the fifteenth century, and reports digital images unavailable.

evidence.citation: British Library, ‘Digitisation services.’
evidence.summary: Provides the reproduction-order route and explains that original manuscripts are photographed subject to assessment and quotation.

evidence.citation: British Library Imaging Services, digitisation rate card.
evidence.summary: Official rate card showing that manuscript imaging is fee-based and quotation-dependent.

evidence.citation: British Library, ‘Terms and conditions: Imaging Services.’
evidence.summary: States terms governing supplied files and distinguishes image supply from permissions and reuse conditions.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, ‘Literary manuscripts.’
evidence.summary: Identifies Gg.4.27 among CUL literary manuscripts and provides an institutional access route to catalog information.

evidence.citation: M. R. James, description of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(1), Cambridge repository PDF.
evidence.locator: Gg.4.27(1): extent, dimensions, contents, Troilus leaves, lacunae, supplied leaves
evidence.summary: Describes the 517-leaf medieval codex plus 36 supplied leaves, its physical format and early fifteenth-century date, the broad Troilus and Canterbury Tales boundaries, 62 missing whole leaves, and the later supplied sequence.

evidence.citation: ‘Female Voices, French Frames: MS Gg.4.27,’ in The Reception of Chaucer's Shorter Poems, 1400–1450, Cambridge University Press.
evidence.summary: Provides the modern 1410–1425 dating and East Anglian association for the principal copying phase; this witness attribution is not transferred to Chaucer's composition place.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory.
evidence.summary: Documents CUL's bespoke cultural-heritage imaging service and directs users to the request portal and contact route.

evidence.citation: Cambridge University Library, imaging and licensing request guide.
evidence.summary: Explains the request and licensing workflow for images and permissions, including the need to identify intended use.

evidence.citation: University of Texas at San Antonio Chaucer catalog, Poetical Works: A Facsimile of Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27; corroborated by CiNii Books record BA01073546.
evidence.summary: Confirms the existence, three-volume form, Parkes and Beadle introductions, D. S. Brewer/Pilgrim publication, and catalog access routes for the modern printed manuscript facsimile; records vary on the terminal publication year.

evidence.citation: U.S. Copyright Office, Circular 15A, Duration of Copyright (rev. April 2026).
evidence.locator: Page 2, works first published in the United States
evidence.summary: Provides the official U.S. term framework used to classify the nineteenth-century editions as public domain and to leave the 1967 reprint's possible new matter unresolved pending a work-specific analysis.

open_questions.question: Did U-M key CME00002 directly from a physical 1967 Johnson Reprint, or from an intermediate file or copy derived from that reprint?
next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production metadata or scan/keying documentation.
Compare systematic defects in the XML against HathiTrust's Johnson volume and scans of the original parts.

open_questions.question: Can a public or repository-supplied complete physical-object facsimile be obtained for Morgan M.817, Harley 2280, and Gg.4.27, including bindings, flyleaves, blanks, and both present Gg bindings?
next_steps: Request complete-codex quotations from the Morgan, British Library, and Cambridge rather than requesting only Troilus leaves.

For any supplied object, record the full file set and then map the work portion separately: Morgan current fols. 1r–approximately 119v; Harley fols. 1r–98r; Gg broad region fols. 14r–131v with Furnivall's extant medieval stream ending at 126v.
Recheck the British Library ARK periodically for restored digital images.
notes: The Morgan's selected fol. 1r image and the public CUL catalog PDF are not complete manuscript facsimiles.
The printed Gg facsimile may answer many visual questions, but it is not a verified public digital source and may not reproduce every physical-object surface.

open_questions.question: Does Furnivall's historical leaf 120 correspond exactly to current Morgan fol. 119v, and where does the poem end relative to current fol. 120?
next_steps: Verify the end of the work against a complete Morgan surrogate or repository foliation statement.
Preserve both historical and current locators in any future work-portion mapping.

open_questions.question: What exact manuscript features underlie each internal italic span in Furnivall's three transcriptions?
next_steps: Locate any fuller Furnivall convention statement in Chaucer Society paratexts or correspondence.
Compare representative italic spans against complete manuscript images, recording whether each reflects expansion, supplied text, correction, or another feature.
Do not expand the medium-confidence generalization into a locus-level claim without image evidence.

open_questions.question: Does the 1967 Johnson Reprint contain copyrightable new matter or a still-relevant copyright notice or registration beyond the public-domain 1881–1882 edition?
next_steps: Inspect every preliminary and terminal leaf of the HathiTrust volume for notices and added matter.
If reuse of Johnson-specific pages is planned, complete a registration and renewal analysis rather than relying only on the age of Furnivall's edition.
